concepts.title: Diffraction at Single Slit
concepts.conceptBody:
Diffraction at a Single Slit 

Consider a narrow slit $\text{AB}$ of width $\text{d}$ kept perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Lat a plane wave front of monochromatic light be incident normally on this slit. As the width $\text{d}$ of the slit is comparable to the wavelength $\lambda $ of the light, the light gets diffracted on passing through the slit. The converging lens ${{\text{L}}_{2}}$ helps in focusing the diffraction pattern on the screen. The diffraction pattern consists of a bright band at its center $\left( \text{O} \right)$ with alternate dark and bright bands on both sides. The intensity of the bright bands decreases very sharply as one move away from $\text{O}$ on both sides.

Explanation : As the plane wave front ${{\text{W}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{W}}_{2}}$ is incident normally on the slit, the entire slit $\text{AB}$ gets, illuminated. According to Huygen's principle, each point on the portion AB of the wave front becomes the source of secondary disturbance. The secondary wavelets start spreading from $\text{AB}$ in all directions.

concepts.title: Position of Central Maxima
concepts.conceptBody:
Position of Central Maxima 

The secondary wavelets travelling parallel to $\text{MO}$ are focused at $\text{O}$ by the converging lens ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$. Actually, the secondary wavelets originating from any two points equidistant from the center of the slit M (one each in MA and MB) cover equal distances before converging at O. Hence, the path difference between them is zero. As a result, they produce the brightest band at $\text{O}$ on the screen. This is called the central maximum.

concepts.title: Position of Secondary Maxima and Minima
concepts.conceptBody:
Position of Secondary Maxima and Minima

Consider the secondary wave travelling in a direction making an angle $\theta $ with MO. All these waves are focused by lens ${{L}_{1}}$ at point $P$. Such waves start from all the points of slit $AB$ in the same phase but travel different distances in reaching the point $\text{P}$. The intensity at point $\text{P}$ will, therefore, depend on the path difference between the secondary waves emitted from the corresponding points (equidistant from M) of the wave front $\text{AB}$.

Let us calculate the path difference between two secondary waves emitting from points A and B of the wave front. Draw AN perpendicular to the wave coming from B.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Path difference between the secondary wavelets which originate from A and B and reach point $\text{P}$ on the screen, $\text{BN}=\text{ABsin}\theta =\text{dsin}\theta $.

Secondary minima : Let this path difference be equal to $1\lambda $ i.e.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}$ sing $\theta =\lambda $

Hence the path difference between the wavelets originating from points $A$ and $M$ and reaching point $\text{P}$ will be $\lambda /2$. Similarly, the path difference between the wavelets originating from $M$ and $\text{B}$ and reaching $\text{P}$ will also be $\lambda /2$. Thus for each point in the upper half of the slit AM, there exists a corresponding point in its lower half MB such that the wavelets originating from such points reach point $\text{P}$ out of phase i.e. having a phase difference of $\pi $. Therefore, destructive interference takes place at point $\text{P}$ and it represents the first secondary minima.

Similarly, if $\text{dsin}\theta =2\lambda $.

The slit $\text{AB}$ can be imagined to be divided into four equal parts. The path difference between the wavelets originating from corresponding points in two adjacent parts will be $2\lambda /4=\lambda /2$. Hence, the wavelets cause destructive interference and point $\text{P}$ again represents the second minima.

In general, for ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ minima, we have $\text{dsin}\theta =\text{n}\lambda $.

Where $\text{n}=1,2,3,\ldots \ldots $.

Hence the direction of ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ secondary minima

$\text{sin}\theta =\text{n}\frac{\lambda }{\text{d}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ For small values $\theta ,\text{sin}{{\theta }_{n}}={{\theta }_{n}}=n\frac{\lambda }{d}$

Secondary maxima : Let the path difference between the wavelets originating from A and B and reaching an off - point ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$ (not shown in the figure) be $3\lambda /2$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d sin }\theta =3\lambda /2$

We assume that the slit $\text{AB}$ is divided into three equal parts so that the difference between the wavelets originating from the corresponding points in the first two parts is $\lambda /2$. Such wavelets cause destructive interference at ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$. However, the wavelets originating from different point of the third part reinforce each other (not completely as for them, $0>d\text{sin}\theta <\lambda /2$ ) and give rise to the first secondary maxima at ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$. The intensity at the first secondary maxima is much smaller than that of the central maxima.

Similarly, $d\text{sin}\theta =5\lambda /2$ give the second secondary maxima with much lower intensity than the first maxima.

In general, for ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ secondary maxima the have $\text{dsin}\theta =\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\lambda /2$

Where $\text{n}=1,2,3\ldots $

The direction of ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ secondary maxima is

$\text{sin}\theta ={{\theta }_{n}}=\frac{\left( 2n+1 \right)\lambda }{2d}$

Thus, the diffraction pattern due to a single slit consists of central maxima flanked by alternate minima and secondary maxima. The intensity distribution in the pattern on the screen in shown in figure.

The graph shows that as we go away from the central maxima $\text{O}$ on both sides, the intensity of secondary maxima decreases very rapidly.

concepts.title: Width of Central Maxima
concepts.conceptBody:
Width of Central Maxima 

It is the distance between the first secondary minimum an either side of the central maxima Let y be the distance of a first minimum from the center $\left( \text{O} \right)$ of the central maxima.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dsin}\theta =\lambda $ and $\text{sin}\theta =\theta =\frac{\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}}$

Let $f$ be the focal length of the converging lens ${{L}_{2}}$ (placed very close to slit $AB$ ) and $D$ is the distance of the screen from $AB$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D}=\text{f}$

Hence, $\theta =\frac{\text{y}}{\text{f}}=\frac{\text{y}}{\text{D}}$

Comparing equation, we get $\frac{\text{y}}{\text{D}}=\frac{\lambda }{\text{d}},\text{y}=\frac{\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ width of central maxima $=2y=\frac{2\lambda D}{d},y=\frac{2\lambda f}{d}$

Figure shows the widths of the central maxima (CD) and the first secondary maximum (DE or $\text{CF}$ )

Let ${{\text{y}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{y}}_{2}}$ respectively be the distance of the first minima and the second minima from O.

Then width of the first secondary maximum $={{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}=\frac{2\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}}-\frac{\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}}=\frac{\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}}$.

The width of the central maxima is twice the width of the first secondary maximum.

concepts.title: Diffraction Pattern at Single Slit Due to Monochromatic Light and White Light
concepts.conceptBody:
Diffraction Pattern At Single Slit Due To Monochromatic Light and White Light 

When a parallel beam of monochromatic light is incident normally on the slit, the diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of alternate bright and dark bands of equal width. The intensity is maximum at the central bright band and it decreases very rapidly for successive secondary bright bands i.e. maximum.

When the beam of white light is incident on the slit, the diffraction pattern is colored. The central maxima is white but the other bands are colored. As the band width $\propto \lambda $ the red band will be wider than the violet band (as ${{\lambda }_{\text{R}}}>{{\lambda }_{\text{v}}}$ ).

concepts.title: Intensity of Diffraction Pattern at Single Slit
concepts.conceptBody:
Intensity of Single Slit Diffraction Pattern 

We can use phasors to determine the light intensity distribution for a single slit diffraction pattern. The proof is beyond our syllabus, we are just writing here the intensity at angle $\theta $.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} I & ={{I}_{0}}{{\left[ \frac{\text{sin}\beta /2}{\beta /2} \right]}^{2}}\text{ Here }\frac{\beta }{2}=\frac{\pi \text{asin}\theta }{\lambda } \\ \text{ or }I & ={{I}_{0}}{{\left[ \frac{\text{sin}\left( \pi \text{asin}\theta /\lambda \right)}{\left( \pi \text{asin}\theta \right)/\lambda } \right]}^{2}}\left( \text{at }\text{ angle }\theta \right) \\ \end{array}$

Here ${{I}_{0}}$ is the intensity at $\theta =0$ (the central maximum).

From this result we see that minima occurs when, $\frac{\pi \text{asin}\theta }{\lambda }=\text{m}\pi$

$\text{ or }\text{ sin}\theta =\text{m}\frac{\lambda }{\text{a}}\text{ m}=\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots$

which is in agreement with the formula derived earlier

Note : The $\text{sin}\theta =0$, corresponds to central maxima while $\frac{\pi \text{asin}\theta }{\lambda }=\pi $, corresponds to first minima.

concepts.title: Resolving Power of Diffraction Grating
concepts.conceptBody:
Diffraction Grating

It consists of large number of equally spaced parallel slits. If light is incident normally on a transmission grating, the direction of principal maxima is given by.

$\text{dsin}\theta =\text{n}\lambda $

Here $\text{d}$ is the distance between two consecutive slits and is called grating element.

$\text{n}=1,2,3,\ldots $ is the order of principal maxima’s.

Resolving Power of Diffraction Grating

The diffraction grating is most useful for measuring wavelengths accurately. Like the prism, the diffraction grating can be used to disperse a spectrum into its wavelength components. Of the two devices, the grating is the more precise if one wants to distinguish two closely spaced wavelengths. For two nearly equal wavelengths ${{\lambda }_{1}}$ and ${{\lambda }_{2}}$ between which a diffraction grating can just barely distinguish, the resolving power $R$ of the grating is defined as

$\text{R}=\frac{\lambda }{{{\lambda }_{2}}-{{\lambda }_{1}}}=\frac{\lambda }{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\lambda }$

where $\lambda =\left( {{\lambda }_{1}}+{{\lambda }_{2}} \right)/2$ and $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\lambda ={{\lambda }_{2}}-{{\lambda }_{1}}$.
